TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Basic Knowledge

Timeframes

💡 It's post-dinner time and while you're scrolling down your X feed, sitting on your comfy sofa, a few trader's words pop up and catch your attention: “I'm gonna buy toward this region and if the price action shows strength, sell at HTF levels”.

How many times have you read a similar statement? Plenty of times I’m sure, even from me when I’m individuating potential areas of interest, but I never explained what they truly mean. This content deals with both price action and psychological standpoints, which will immensely help you navigate the crypto market. It might get a little difficult at first, but I truly recommend you to push yourself into understanding this, because it will be very important.

I. Timeframes perspective

Logically, HTF stands for “High Timeframes” while LTF stands for “Low Timeframes”.

  • HTFs are generally Daily, Weekly, and Monthly.

These are the classic ones.

  • LTF instead relates to everything below 4H, including 1HR, 15m(minute), 5m and so on.

As you can imagine, these timeframes represent intervals of time in which a trader/investor decides to operate.

  • Scalpers and short-term traders adopt the LTFs.
  • Swing traders and long-term investors the HTFs.

The first ones can guarantee fast profits but they're also more subject to market makers' “tricks”, which can produce false signals and make people lose money due to compulsive buy-and-sell operations. It's for pro traders only. There’s a reason why the herd panics when Bitcoin goes down more than 5% in a day. It's a psychological game that digs into the roots of the human mind. Ever wondered why the masses choose to pick up lottery tickets and gambling?

They don't have the patience to build a strategy that lasts for years, the “get rich slowly” one. They want everything in an instant and for the same reason, LTFs leverage their emotions because they cannot look at the bigger picture.

“Bitcoin is going down, I need to sell” “Bitcoin is going up, I need to buy”

The mind of these people is weak and hasn't been forged, no surprise they lose money. And it’s exactly at this moment that the king can come to save the game: HTF.

Contrary to popular belief, hedge funds and institutions don't look at intraday movements, they don't continuously “flip-flop” their positions : they calculate their performance over longer periods. According to Preqin, hedge funds measure their cumulative performance over three months, one year, three years and more.

This is valid for the legacy markets in which they operate such as stocks, commodities and forex, but also for crypto. They exclusively look at key areas in which the price of an asset can be headed. This makes their work easier, less stressful, but at the same time more powerful, exploiting the divergence between the herd’s mindset and real profits. That’s why HTFs are and will always be king.

II. Individuating timeframes

When it comes to spotting HTF levels, the first thing to do is to look at the past price action to understand which levels have been critical points in which the price has reacted. This includes wicks and bodies of the candles.

Let’s take the weekly chart with 3 HTF levels which have been watershed points during the past years: 32K, 38K and 48K. As you can see from the chart, each level has been characterized by a lot of trading activity:

  • 32K saw Bitcoin retracing from the 38K and using that support as a springboard to jump during the first parabolic impulse in February 2021. Subsequently, after the first drawdown from 65K (April high), 32K has been used to “fish” some liquidity (notice the cluster of 5 wicks) and then as another point to jump back to new highs
  • 38K instead, as we saw before, as a first resistance during January 2021, June 2021 and then as a support in February-March 2022 used by Bitcoin to make the “complacency” impulse toward 48K
  • 48K has been used to grab some liquidity in April 2021, as a resistance in which the price has been congested from August to September 2021, then again in December 2021 and as a macro resistance to stop the “complacency” shoulder run.

The recent price action simply reflects the aftermath of the ETF saga, marked by a noticeable rejection in the market. All HTF levels therefore, have been crucial junctures to determine potential outcomes for the price.

To add another tool for validating HTFs, I use Fibonacci retracements (as you probably noticed in many past analyses) because I firmly think they support the thesis. By taking the same chart and applying Fibonacci retracements, we can see the confluence they usually bring to HTFs (48K/0.618, for example)

Sure, they don’t perfectly match every single HTF level, but if you read Fibonacci Retracements , you’ll see that over time the price will be attracted like a magnet to key Fibonacci levels (understanding the importance of each Fib number is crucial).

III. HTF Liquidity Levels (On-Chain)

During your crypto journey, you probably have encountered the term “Liquidity”, which can be associated with HTF price action levels.

The concept of liquidity levels refers to levels which offer a high concentration of trading activity, indicating strong buying or selling interest without altering the price.

High liquidity on a higher timeframe means therefore that there's a consistent presence of market participants in a specific area that are waiting for the price to reach it.

You can check liquidity levels using these 2 tools: Liquidation levels + HTF liquidity levels, a trading indicator. (this is different from liquidity associated to PA)

1. Liquidation Levels

As said, traders commonly position stops and liquidations of leveraged trades behind support or resistance levels, creating accumulations in specific zones that become significant points of interest. With billions concentrated in these areas, whales can swiftly enter positions without impacting the price. Moreover, these levels exert substantial pressure on one side of the order book, potentially fueling a price reversal.

This Liquidation Heatmap shows us that the most important areas are the 38.500K one and the 41.000K one, clearly notable by the spike in color.

The brighter the color, the more billions are allocated to a specific price region, simple as that. This type of service is usually quite expensive but fortunately, with Coinglass, it is free and you can check it out here → Liquidation Heatmap

Alongside it, I use the Liquidation Map set on 30 days. The liquidation map shows where traders’ positions might be forcibly closed due to price drops. On unregulated exchanges in fact, this poses risks. If many positions get liquidated simultaneously, it can cause big price swings and Institutional players often use these swings to their advantage.

Different combinations of leverage and timeframes create different clusters of potential liquidations, with denser clusters having a bigger impact on prices.

The X-axis represents the mark price while the Y-axis the increase of liquidations. (don’t look at the red arrow because the first time I looked at it I didn't understand anything).

The liquidation chart shows how much a specific position will be impacted when the price hits a certain level. Greater "liquidation bars" signal a more pronounced reaction because of increased liquidity.

2. Liquidity Levels

You know my passion for charting and from it came the necessity to search for something liquidity-related to integrate into my charts. Fortunately, I’ve found a very useful indicator made from “sbtnc” which matches this need: “HTF Liquidity Levels”.

This indicator, for the concept of liquidity we explored before, shows all the daily, weekly and monthly untapped levels (in which therefore there are clusters of liquidity) providing an additional insight when charting. To apply it, search for “HTF liquidity levels” by sbtnc and click on it.

At this point, your chart will be something like this: All the red and green bars are untapped levels. The larger the bar, the bigger the untapped level, simple as that. Giving a little nod, you can see that all the bars match a specific HTF price level, making it easier to spot important areas.

Look at the rejection from 48K.. a coincidence there was a bar there? The wick of the candle has in fact spiked until reaching the bar. That's how I mainly use this indicator, as a confirmation for my main thesis. (as every indicator of course)

3. HTF + News... Coincidence?

For how “conspiracy theorist” this may seem, it is never a coincidence that news is released exactly at HTF levels, NEVER.

Indeed, the mental game is quite easy and despite its easiness, it still reaps thousands of victims.

Before and coinciding with a significant HTF, a large wave of news is deliberately released to steer the herd in a particular direction that aligns with the prevailing trend. Coincidence that the ETF news has dominated the scene while Bitcoin was approaching 48K?

Coincidence that the FTX news was constantly released while Bitcoin was on its way to finding the bottom at 15.8K? This made sure that the herd was buying the local top and selling the absolute bottom, at an advantage for market makers.

Remember: the market tends to bottom during max FUD news and tends to top during max FOMO news. It will be easier for you to spot key areas basing your thesis on the intensification of news (bullish/bearish ones) together with the approach on HTFs. Simple psychological trick that always do the work based on sheep mindset.

Conclusion

HTFs are daily / weekly / monthly price action levels that provide a broader perspective on price movements and trends compared to shorter timeframes like hourly or 15-minute charts (noise).

They include significant key price zones that have historical significance in the price movement of an asset that can indicate potential areas of price reversal or continuation.

What to do:

  • Switch your chart to daily/weekly/monthly (the higher the timeframe the stronger the signal)
  • Look for these historical levels if you want to gauge potential buy and sell areas, considering not only the candle but also the wick (as per the 48K example)
  • Match them with liquidity levels to have a better perspective/confirmation
  • Match them with news
  • Look for HTF closures above/below these levels
  • Take profit/buy more dependently of the closure
Important note: liquidity levels can be useful but aren't the perfect and ultimate tool. Use them exclusively as confirmation for your main thesis by looking at the price action. To conclude, I think this the easiest way to navigate the market and make informed decisions without caring too much about short/term volatility. You'll discover along the way how powerful this approach will be, helping you to filter the noise and find “sweet spots” to sell/buy.
